[[arz:تينريو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Tenryū-ji (Kyoto)]][[cs:Tenrjúdži]][[de:Tenryū-ji]][[en:Tenryū-ji]][[es:Tenryū-ji]][[fa:تنریو-جی]][[fi:Tenryū-ji]][[fr:Tenryū-ji]][[it:Tenryū-ji]][[ja:天龍寺]][[ko:덴류지]][[my:တန်းရူးဂျိ]][[nl:Tenryu-ji]][[pl:Tenryū-ji]][[ro:Tenryū-ji]][[th:เท็นรีวจิ]][[tr:Tenryū-ji]][[vi:Tenryū-ji]][[zh:天龍寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tenryū-ji school|qid=Q126510414}}|prefecture={{ill|Ukyō Ward|qid=Q580186}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.01599|N|135.67379|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji|2=ja|3=足利尊氏|qid=Q297107|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q297107}}; {{ill|Musō Soseki|qid=Q384703}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|qid=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kyoto Gozan|qid=Q8193432}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1759226]] Wikidata name: Tenryū-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Major temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tenryū-ji
天龍寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = Sōgenchi Garden | caption = Sōgenchi Garden, a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 68 Saga-Tenryūji Susukinobabachō, [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|57.47|N|135|40|25.58|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tenryū-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tenryuji.com/ | founded_by = [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]] | year_completed = 1345 }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū-ji'''|天龍寺}}, formally known as {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū Shiseizen-ji'''|天龍資聖禅寺}}, is the head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] sect of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], located in Susukinobaba-chō, Ukyō Ward, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The temple was founded by [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]]. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountains]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]], founded a temple called [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and his son [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. Ashikaga became the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]] the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, [[:en:Ryakuō|Ryakuō]] being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Tadayoshi]] supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called [[:en:Tenryūji-bune|Tenryūji-bune]] were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a [[:en:Tribute|tributary]] relationship with the Imperial Court of [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming-dynasty]] China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] refused to submit to Chinese [[:en:Suzerainty|Suzerainty]]. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in [[:en:Kamakura period|the Kamakura period]] style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of [[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]] and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *[[:en:List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism''.] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. :''Much of the content of this article was translated from the equivalent Japanese-language article, accessed on July 24, 2006.'' ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2= Young |first2= Michiko |title= The Art of the Japanese Garden |location= Singapore |publisher= Tuttle |date= 2005 |type= Hardback |isbn=978-0-8048-3598-5}} ==External links== {{Commons and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/10tenryu.html Tenryū-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20110610012511/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/earth/photos/japan/tenryuji-garden-cc-sofafort.jpg image of Tenryū-ji garden] {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhist temples 天龍寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = Sōgenchi Garden | caption = Sōgenchi Garden, a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 68 Saga-Tenryūji Susukinobabachō, [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|57.47|N|135|40|25.58|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tenryū-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tenryuji.com/ | founded_by = [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]] | year_completed = 1345 }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū-ji'''|天龍寺}}, formally known as {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū Shiseizen-ji'''|天龍資聖禅寺}}, is the head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] sect of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], located in Susukinobaba-chō, Ukyō Ward, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The temple was founded by [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]]. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountains]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]], founded a temple called [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and his son [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. Ashikaga became the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]] the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, [[:en:Ryakuō|Ryakuō]] being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Tadayoshi]] supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called [[:en:Tenryūji-bune|Tenryūji-bune]] were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a [[:en:Tribute|tributary]] relationship with the Imperial Court of [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming-dynasty]] China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] refused to submit to Chinese [[:en:Suzerainty|Suzerainty]]. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in [[:en:Kamakura period|the Kamakura period]] style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of [[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]] and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *[[:en:List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism''.] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. :''Much of the content of this article was translated from the equivalent Japanese-language article, accessed on July 24, 2006.'' ==Further reading== *{{cite book David |last2= Young |first2= Michiko |title= The Art of the Japanese Garden |location= Singapore |publisher= Tuttle |date= 2005 |type= Hardback |isbn=978-0-8048-3598-5}} ==External links== {{Commons [[arz:تينريو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Tenryū-ji (Kyoto)]][[cs:Tenrjúdži]][[de:Tenryū-ji]][[en:Tenryū-ji]][[es:Tenryū-ji]][[fa:تنریو-جی]][[fi:Tenryū-ji]][[fr:Tenryū-ji]][[it:Tenryū-ji]][[ja:天龍寺]][[ko:덴류지]][[my:တန်းရူးဂျိ]][[nl:Tenryu-ji]][[pl:Tenryū-ji]][[ro:Tenryū-ji]][[th:เท็นรีวจิ]][[tr:Tenryū-ji]][[vi:Tenryū-ji]][[zh:天龍寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tenryū-ji school|WD=Q126510414}}|prefecture={{ill|Ukyō Ward|WD=Q580186}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.01599|N|135.67379|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji|2=ja|3=足利尊氏|WD=Q297107|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q297107}}; {{ill|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kyoto Gozan|WD=Q8193432}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1759226]] Wikidata name: Tenryū-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Major temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tenryū-ji
天龍寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = Sōgenchi Garden | caption = Sōgenchi Garden, a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 68 Saga-Tenryūji Susukinobabachō, [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|57.47|N|135|40|25.58|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tenryū-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tenryuji.com/ | founded_by = [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]] | year_completed = 1345 }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū-ji'''|天龍寺}}, formally known as {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū Shiseizen-ji'''|天龍資聖禅寺}}, is the head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] sect of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], located in Susukinobaba-chō, Ukyō Ward, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The temple was founded by [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]]. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountains]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]], founded a temple called [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and his son [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. Ashikaga became the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]] the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, [[:en:Ryakuō|Ryakuō]] being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Tadayoshi]] supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called [[:en:Tenryūji-bune|Tenryūji-bune]] were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a [[:en:Tribute|tributary]] relationship with the Imperial Court of [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming-dynasty]] China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] refused to submit to Chinese [[:en:Suzerainty|Suzerainty]]. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in [[:en:Kamakura period|the Kamakura period]] style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of [[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]] and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *[[:en:List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. [[arz:تينريو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Tenryū-ji (Kyoto)]][[cs:Tenrjúdži]][[de:Tenryū-ji]][[en:Tenryū-ji]][[es:Tenryū-ji]][[fa:تنریو-جی]][[fi:Tenryū-ji]][[fr:Tenryū-ji]][[it:Tenryū-ji]][[ja:天龍寺]][[ko:덴류지]][[my:တန်းရူးဂျိ]][[nl:Tenryu-ji]][[pl:Tenryū-ji]][[ro:Tenryū-ji]][[th:เท็นรีวจิ]][[tr:Tenryū-ji]][[vi:Tenryū-ji]][[zh:天龍寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tenryū-ji school|WD=Q126510414}}|prefecture={{ill|Ukyō Ward|WD=Q580186}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.01599|N|135.67379|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji|2=ja|3=足利尊氏|WD=Q297107|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q297107}}; {{ill|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kyoto Gozan|WD=Q8193432}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1759226]] Wikidata name: Tenryū-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Major temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tenryū-ji
天龍寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = Sōgenchi Garden | caption = Sōgenchi Garden, a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 68 Saga-Tenryūji Susukinobabachō, [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|57.47|N|135|40|25.58|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tenryū-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tenryuji.com/ | founded_by = [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]] | year_completed = 1345 }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū-ji'''|天龍寺}}, formally known as {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū Shiseizen-ji'''|天龍資聖禅寺}}, is the head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] sect of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], located in Susukinobaba-chō, Ukyō Ward, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The temple was founded by [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]]. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountains]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]], founded a temple called [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and his son [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. Ashikaga became the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]] the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, [[:en:Ryakuō|Ryakuō]] being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Tadayoshi]] supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called [[:en:Tenryūji-bune|Tenryūji-bune]] were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a [[:en:Tribute|tributary]] relationship with the Imperial Court of [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming-dynasty]] China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] refused to submit to Chinese [[:en:Suzerainty|Suzerainty]]. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in [[:en:Kamakura period|the Kamakura period]] style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of [[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]] and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *[[:en:List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism''.] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. :''Much of the content of this article was translated from the equivalent Japanese-language article, accessed on July 24, 2006.'' ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2= Young |first2= Michiko |title= The Art of the Japanese Garden |location= Singapore |publisher= Tuttle |date= 2005 |type= Hardback |isbn=978-0-8048-3598-5}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Tenryū-ji (Kyoto)}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20090923153635/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/w_heritage/12/ Tenryū-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/10tenryu.html Tenryū-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20110610012511/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/earth/photos/japan/tenryuji-garden-cc-sofafort.jpg image of Tenryū-ji garden] {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhist temples 天龍寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = Sōgenchi Garden | caption = Sōgenchi Garden, a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 68 Saga-Tenryūji Susukinobabachō, [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|57.47|N|135|40|25.58|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tenryū-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tenryuji.com/ | founded_by = [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]] | year_completed = 1345 }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū-ji'''|天龍寺}}, formally known as {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū Shiseizen-ji'''|天龍資聖禅寺}}, is the head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] sect of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], located in Susukinobaba-chō, Ukyō Ward, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The temple was founded by [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]]. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountains]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]], founded a temple called [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and his son [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. Ashikaga became the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]] the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, [[:en:Ryakuō|Ryakuō]] being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Tadayoshi]] supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called [[:en:Tenryūji-bune|Tenryūji-bune]] were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a [[:en:Tribute|tributary]] relationship with the Imperial Court of [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming-dynasty]] China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] refused to submit to Chinese [[:en:Suzerainty|Suzerainty]]. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in [[:en:Kamakura period|the Kamakura period]] style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of [[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]] and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *[[:en:List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism''.] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. [[arz:تينريو جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Tenryū-ji (Kyoto)]][[cs:Tenrjúdži]][[de:Tenryū-ji]][[en:Tenryū-ji]][[es:Tenryū-ji]][[fa:تنریو-جی]][[fi:Tenryū-ji]][[fr:Tenryū-ji]][[it:Tenryū-ji]][[ja:天龍寺]][[ko:덴류지]][[my:တန်းရူးဂျိ]][[nl:Tenryu-ji]][[pl:Tenryū-ji]][[ro:Tenryū-ji]][[th:เท็นรีวจิ]][[tr:Tenryū-ji]][[vi:Tenryū-ji]][[zh:天龍寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tenryū-ji school|WD=Q126510414}}|prefecture={{ill|Ukyō Ward|WD=Q580186}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.01599|N|135.67379|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji|2=ja|3=足利尊氏|WD=Q297107|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q297107}}; {{ill|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|WD=Q874027}}; {{ill|Kyoto Gozan|WD=Q8193432}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1759226]] Wikidata name: Tenryū-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Major temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tenryū-ji
天龍寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = Sōgenchi Garden | caption = Sōgenchi Garden, a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 68 Saga-Tenryūji Susukinobabachō, [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|57.47|N|135|40|25.58|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tenryū-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tenryuji.com/ | founded_by = [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] and [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]] | year_completed = 1345 }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū-ji'''|天龍寺}}, formally known as {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū Shiseizen-ji'''|天龍資聖禅寺}}, is the head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch of the [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]] sect of [[:en:Zen|Zen]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]], located in Susukinobaba-chō, Ukyō Ward, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The temple was founded by [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]]. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]], the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountains]]. In 1994, it was registered as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]], founded a temple called [[:en:Danrin-ji|Danrin-ji]] on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and his son [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]], who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. Ashikaga became the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]] the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, [[:en:Ryakuō|Ryakuō]] being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Tadayoshi]] supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called [[:en:Tenryūji-bune|Tenryūji-bune]] were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a [[:en:Tribute|tributary]] relationship with the Imperial Court of [[:en:Ming dynasty|Ming-dynasty]] China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]] refused to submit to Chinese [[:en:Suzerainty|Suzerainty]]. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in [[:en:Kamakura period|the Kamakura period]] style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] and [[:en:Emperor Kameyama|Emperor Kameyama]] also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by [[:en:Musō Soseki|Musō Soseki]], features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]] of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of [[:en:Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara]] and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *[[:en:List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[:en:Japanese garden|Japanese garden]] == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon.
