天龍寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = Sōgenchi Garden | caption = Sōgenchi Garden, a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 68 Saga-Tenryūji Susukinobabachō, {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|57.47|N|135|40|25.58|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tenryū-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)|1=Five Mountain System}} | website = http://www.tenryuji.com/ | founded_by = {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}} | year_completed = 1345 }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū-ji'''|天龍寺}}, formally known as {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū Shiseizen-ji'''|天龍資聖禅寺}}, is the head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} sect of {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, located in Susukinobaba-chō, Ukyō Ward, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The temple was founded by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}, the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountains|1=Five Mountain System}}. In 1994, it was registered as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, as part of the "{{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|WD=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga|1=Emperor Saga}}, founded a temple called {{ill|Danrin-ji|en|Danrin-ji|WD=Q3015355|lt=Danrin-ji|1=Danrin-ji}} on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga|1=Emperor Go-Saga}} and his son {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. Ashikaga became the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino|1=Yoshino, Nara}} the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, {{ill|Ryakuō|en|Ryakuō|WD=Q1146182|lt=Ryakuō|1=Ryakuō}} being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|WD=Q725375|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}} supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called {{ill|Tenryūji-bune|en|Tenryūji-bune|WD=Q958392|lt=Tenryūji-bune|1=Tenryūji-bune}} were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a {{ill|Tribute|en|Tribute|WD=Q748289|lt=tributary|1=Tribute}} relationship with the Imperial Court of {{ill|Ming dynasty|en|Ming dynasty|WD=Q9903|lt=Ming-dynasty|1=Ming dynasty}} China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}} refused to submit to Chinese {{ill|Suzerainty|en|Suzerainty|WD=Q463573|lt=Suzerainty|1=Suzerainty}}. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=the Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga|1=Emperor Go-Saga}} and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism''.] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin|1=Heinrich Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom|1=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. :''Much of the content of this article was translated from the equivalent Japanese-language article, accessed on July 24, 2006.'' ==Further reading== *{{cite book 天龍寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Tenryuji Kyoto.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = Sōgenchi Garden | caption = Sōgenchi Garden, a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 68 Saga-Tenryūji Susukinobabachō, {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|57.47|N|135|40|25.58|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tenryū-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)|1=Five Mountain System}} | website = http://www.tenryuji.com/ | founded_by = {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}} | year_completed = 1345 }} {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū-ji'''|天龍寺}}, formally known as {{Nihongo|'''Tenryū Shiseizen-ji'''|天龍資聖禅寺}}, is the head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} sect of {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, located in Susukinobaba-chō, Ukyō Ward, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The temple was founded by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}, the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountains|1=Five Mountain System}}. In 1994, it was registered as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, as part of the "{{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|WD=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga|1=Emperor Saga}}, founded a temple called {{ill|Danrin-ji|en|Danrin-ji|WD=Q3015355|lt=Danrin-ji|1=Danrin-ji}} on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga|1=Emperor Go-Saga}} and his son {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. Ashikaga became the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino|1=Yoshino, Nara}} the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, {{ill|Ryakuō|en|Ryakuō|WD=Q1146182|lt=Ryakuō|1=Ryakuō}} being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|WD=Q725375|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}} supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called {{ill|Tenryūji-bune|en|Tenryūji-bune|WD=Q958392|lt=Tenryūji-bune|1=Tenryūji-bune}} were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a {{ill|Tribute|en|Tribute|WD=Q748289|lt=tributary|1=Tribute}} relationship with the Imperial Court of {{ill|Ming dynasty|en|Ming dynasty|WD=Q9903|lt=Ming-dynasty|1=Ming dynasty}} China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}} refused to submit to Chinese {{ill|Suzerainty|en|Suzerainty|WD=Q463573|lt=Suzerainty|1=Suzerainty}}. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=the Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga|1=Emperor Go-Saga}} and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism''.] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin|1=Heinrich Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom|1=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. :''Much of the content of this article was translated from the equivalent Japanese-language article, accessed on July 24, 2006.'' ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2= Young |first2= Michiko |title= The Art of the Japanese Garden |location= Singapore |publisher= Tuttle |date= 2005 |type= Hardback |isbn=978-0-8048-3598-5}} ==External links== {{Commons The temple was founded by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} in 1339, primarily to venerate [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Gautama Buddha]], and its first chief priest was {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}. Construction was completed in 1345. As a temple related to both the Ashikaga family and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}, the temple is held in high esteem, and is Ranked number one among Kyoto's so-called {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountains|1=Five Mountain System}}. In 1994, it was registered as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, as part of the "{{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|WD=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}". == History == [[File:TenryujiBuilding.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] on the grounds of Tenryū-ji]] In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, Empress [[Tachibana no Kachiko]], wife of {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga|1=Emperor Saga}}, founded a temple called {{ill|Danrin-ji|en|Danrin-ji|WD=Q3015355|lt=Danrin-ji|1=Danrin-ji}} on the site of present-day Tenryū-ji. The temple fell into disrepair over the next four hundred years. In the mid-thirteenth century, {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga|1=Emperor Go-Saga}} and his son {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} turned the area into an imperial villa which they called {{Nihongo|"Kameyama Detached Palace"|亀山殿|Kameyama-dono}}. The name "Kameyama", which literally means "turtle mountain", was selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura, which lies to the west of Tenryū-ji—it is said to be similar to the shape of a turtle's shell. All Japanese temples constructed after the Nara period have a ''sangō'', a mountain name used as an honorary prefix. Tenryū-ji's ''sangō'', {{Nihongo|''Reigizan''|霊亀山||"mountain of the spirit turtle"}}, was also selected due to the shape of Mt. Ogura. The palace was converted into a temple in the middle of the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 197. at the behest of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, who wished to use the temple to hold a memorial service for {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. Ashikaga became the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' in 1338, and Go-Daigo died in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino|1=Yoshino, Nara}} the following year. Ashikaga opposed the failed [[Kenmu Restoration]], which was started by Emperor Go-Daigo, and the emperor decreed that Ashikaga be hunted down and executed. When his former-friend-turned-enemy died, Ashikaga recommended that Zen monk Musō Soseki construct a temple for his memorial service. It is said that the temple was originally going to be named {{Nihongo|''Ryakuō Shiseizen-ji''|暦応資聖禅寺}}, {{ill|Ryakuō|en|Ryakuō|WD=Q1146182|lt=Ryakuō|1=Ryakuō}} being the name of the reign of the emperor of the northern court at that time. However, Ashikaga Takauji's younger brother, {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|WD=Q725375|lt=Tadayoshi|1=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}} supposedly had a dream about a golden dragon flitting about the Ōi River (also known as the Hozu River), which lies south of the temple, and the temple was instead named Tenryū Shiseizen-ji—the term "Tenryū" literally means "dragon of the sky". In order to raise the funds to build the temple, two trading vessels called {{ill|Tenryūji-bune|en|Tenryūji-bune|WD=Q958392|lt=Tenryūji-bune|1=Tenryūji-bune}} were launched in 1342. A ceremony was held on the seventh anniversary of Emperor Daigo II's death in 1345, which functioned as both a celebration of the completion of the temple, and as Daigo's memorial. During the 1430s, the temple entered into a {{ill|Tribute|en|Tribute|WD=Q748289|lt=tributary|1=Tribute}} relationship with the Imperial Court of {{ill|Ming dynasty|en|Ming dynasty|WD=Q9903|lt=Ming-dynasty|1=Ming dynasty}} China. Chinese imperial policy at the time forbade formal trade outside of the [[Sinocentrism#Sinocentric system|Sinocentric world order]], and both the Japanese imperial court and {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}} refused to submit to Chinese {{ill|Suzerainty|en|Suzerainty|WD=Q463573|lt=Suzerainty|1=Suzerainty}}. This arrangement with the Tenryū-ji allowed for formal trade to be undertaken between the two countries, in exchange for China's control over the succession of chief abbot of the temple.Kerr, George H. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA137 ''Okinawa: The History of an Island People'', pp. 137]–139. This arrangement gave the Zen sect, and Tenryū-ji more specifically, a near monopoly on Japan's legitimate trade with China. In conjunction with the temple of the same name in Okinawa, as well as other Zen temples there, Tenryū-ji priests and monks played major roles in coordinating the China–Okinawa–Japan tradeKerr, [https://books.google.com/books?id=vaAKJQyzpLkC&q=Okinawa:+The+History+of+an+Island+People&pg=PA100 p. 100.] through to the 19th century. The temple prospered as the most important Rinzai temple in Kyoto, and the temple grounds grew to roughly {{convert|330,000|m2|ha acre|adj=off|sp=us|abbr=off}} in size, extending all the way to present-day Katabira-no-Tsuji station on the [[:en:Keifuku Railway|Keifuku Railway]]. At one time, the massive grounds were said to contain some 150 sub-temples, however, the temple was plagued with numerous fires, and all of the original buildings have been destroyed. During the Middle Ages, the temple met with fire six times: in 1358, 1367, 1373, 1380, 1447 and 1467. The temple was destroyed again during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} and subsequently rebuilt, but in 1815 it was lost to yet another fire. The temple was severely damaged during the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] of 1864, and most of the buildings as they stand today are reconstructions from the latter half of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. The garden to the west of the abbey, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, shows only traces of its original design. == Layout == On the eastern boundary of the temple grounds lie two gates: {{Nihongo|Chokushi Gate|勅使門|chokushimon}} and {{Nihongo|Middle Gate|中門|chūmon}}, from which the path to the temple itself leads west. Generally, Zen temple grounds are designed so that they face the south, with major buildings aligned along the north-south axis. Tenryū-ji's layout is an exception to this principle. Sub-temples line both sides of the path, which leads to the lecture hall. There are numerous buildings behind the lecture hall, such as {{Nihongo|large abbey|大方丈|ōhōjō}}, the {{Nihongo|small abbey|小方丈|kohōjō}}, the kitchen, the meditation hall, and {{Nihongo|Tahō-den|多宝殿}} hall, however, each of these is a modern reconstruction. * Chokushi gate is a one-storey gate, constructed in Yotsuashimon style. It is the oldest structure on the temple grounds and is representative of the style of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. * The teaching hall is located at the center of the temple grounds, which is unusual for a Zen temple. The extant version is a 1900 reconstruction. It contains an image of Gautama Buddha, flanked by two guardians. The decorative painting of a dragon on the ceiling called {{Nihongo|Unryū-zu|雲龍図||lit. "image of the cloud dragon"}} is the work of Suzuki Shōnen. * Ōhōjō was constructed in 1899. * Kohōjō was constructed in 1924. * Tahō-den was constructed in 1934. Although it is a modern building, it was constructed in {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=the Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} style. It contains a wooden image of Emperor Go-Daigo. * Kuri The tombs of {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga|1=Emperor Go-Saga}} and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} also lie within the temple grounds. File:Tenryuji Kyoto37n4380.jpg|Chokushi gate File:Tenryuji Kyoto36n4500.jpg|The teaching hall File:Tenryuji Kyoto02s3s4500.jpg|Ōhōjō File:Tenryuji Kyoto08n4592.jpg|Tahō-den == Cultural properties == [[File:Tenryuji Kyoto41n4592.jpg|thumb|The Sōgen Pond, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, is one of the highlights of the temple complex.]] The garden, created by {{ill|Musō Soseki|en|Musō Soseki|WD=Q384703|lt=Musō Soseki|1=Musō Soseki}}, features a circular promenade around {{Nihongo|Sōgen Pond|曹源池|sōgenchi}} and is designated as a [[:en:Special Places of Scenic Beauty|Special Place of Scenic Beauty of Japan]]. The {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} of Tenryū-ji include: * Three portraits of Musō Soseki, and paintings of {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} and Seiryō Hōgen Zenji/Yunmen Daishi * The wooden carving of Gautama Buddha, * Illustrations and writings in the document archive, such as {{Nihongo|Shanaingoryō-ezu|遮那院御領絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōkoshokyōkanji-no-ezu|往古諸郷館地之絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Ōeikinmyō-ezu|応永鈞命絵図}}, {{Nihongo|Tōryōeiyo-bakuseki|東陵永與墨蹟}}, and writings of [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]]. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine.
